content: The most common mistake beginners make when implementing the visitor pattern in Rust is confusing the direction of data flow, specifically by treating the object being visited as the source of truth for dispatch logic rather than using a dedicated visitor type. This happens because documentation often presents traits like impl<T: Trait> Visit<T>, which suggests that every concrete item in an AST or data structure can simply implement a trait to handle its own visitors, leading developers to write generic functions on the host types instead of composing separate visitor structs or enums. The fundamental error is assuming that double dispatch works by having each node type decide who visits it based on runtime checks; in reality, Rust's borrow checker and type system enforce compile-time single dispatch where the visitor type determines which method variant gets called when iterating over a collection like Vec<Node>. When you try to implement a generic visit function on every node type using trait objects or dynamic traits without concrete bindings, you end up with code that looks correct but fails at runtime due to unsoundness or simply never executes because the compiler optimizes it away if the trait bounds are too loose. The correct approach requires shifting your mental model from thinking about how nodes respond to visitors to thinking about how a single visitor type responds to different node types by implementing specific methods for each concrete variant, such as impl Visit for LeafNode and impl Visit for BranchNode, which allows you to walk an entire tree using one cohesive traversal strategy without duplicating logic. This pattern becomes especially critical when walking an Abstract Syntax Tree where you might need to compute statistics across all nodes or perform transformations that depend on the context of sibling nodes; if your visitor methods are not properly typed and do not capture state from previous siblings, your AST walker will produce incorrect results even though it compiles successfully. The performance implication of this mistake is significant because introducing dynamic dispatch through trait objects for every node type increases memory allocation overhead and prevents the compiler from generating efficient code, whereas using a dedicated enum-based visitor like VisitorEnum where each arm contains the specific logic for that node type ensures zero-cost abstractions and allows inlining across all branches. You should never rely on generic functions to handle polymorphism in this context; instead, define your visitor as a struct containing state and methods, then implement those methods explicitly for each concrete node variant so that Rust can optimize away virtual dispatch entirely. When combining this with crates like syn or derive macros for parsing, the visitor pattern provides the only safe way to walk complex hierarchies without breaking type safety, ensuring that every operation on the tree is verified at compile time and never relies on hidden runtime behavior that could silently corrupt your data structure during deep nesting levels.
